headline: War risk goes live: the US and Iran trade strikes, oil rockets toward $80 and Hormuz shipping halts — Indian stocks suffer their worst day in months, the rupee hits a record low, and TCS opens earnings season tonight into the storm.
market_story: Yesterday's scare became today's shock. After Iran struck three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for about a fifth of the world's oil — the US hit more than 80 targets across Iran and 60-plus Revolutionary Guard boats, Iran fired back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over.' Oil did what it always does when that waterway is threatened: US crude (WTI) leapt about 4.6% to ~$73.7 and Brent pushed toward $80, while tanker traffic through Hormuz 'essentially stopped.' The pain flows straight to India, which imports ~85% of its oil and routes roughly 40% of its crude, 60% of its gas and 90% of its cooking gas through that one strait — so the Nifty crashed 2.1% to 23,882, the Sensex sank 1,677 points to 76,504 (the worst session in over three months), banks led the fall (Bank Nifty -2.5%), and the rupee tumbled 59 paise to a record-low 95.55 per dollar. Two other forces made the day worse: the Federal Reserve's first minutes under new chair Kevin Warsh revealed a split committee more worried about inflation than rate cuts — a hawkish tilt that firmed the dollar and, counter-intuitively, dragged gold down 1.5% to ~$4,084 even on a war day (higher-for-longer rates raise the cost of holding metal that pays no interest). The one green shoot was in chips: a report that China will let its top AI firms buy limited quantities of Nvidia's H200 lifted Nvidia ~3% against an otherwise red US tape. Among your favorites, Apple ($314.03, +1.08%) actually rose to a fresh record-area high as money hid in mega-cap quality; Nvidia ($203.05, +3.11%) was the day's standout on the China news and got upgraded to Buy; Reliance (~₹1,275.9, ~-1.3%) slid to a fresh 52-week-low zone as the selloff hit and higher crude squeezes refining margins; and Bitcoin (~$62,096, -1.9%) sank toward one-year lows as risk-off, ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed kept the downtrend intact. All of this lands as TCS reports Q1 results tonight — the market's first earnings reality-check, arriving on its ugliest day in months.

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: US large-caps slipped 0.36% as the Iran shock and rising oil offset a bounce in chips — the broad market resilient but lower, still in its uptrend above the 50/200-day averages.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: The tech-heavy index pared its losses to near-flat, cushioned by a ~3% jump in Nvidia on the China H200 news even as oil and war fears weighed elsewhere.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: The Dow led the US decline (-1.04%, ~500 points) — the old-economy, oil-sensitive index feels a crude spike and risk-off mood more than the tech-led Nasdaq.

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India's benchmark crashed 2.1% and lost the 24,000 mark — the cheap-oil tailwind that drove its rally flipped into a headwind as crude spiked on the US-Iran war.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: The 30-stock index tanked 1,677 points, its worst day in over three months, as the oil shock hit an import-dependent market hardest — intraday it was down as much as 1,921 points.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks led the rout (-2.5%) — a higher oil price and record-low rupee revive inflation worries and trim the odds of the RBI rate cuts that lift lenders.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: Slid further toward a one-year low as the risk-off tape, steady ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed kept the downtrend intact — crypto trading as a risk asset, not a haven.

board.name: Ethereum
board.read: Fell in step with Bitcoin (-1.9%) — the higher-beta crypto stays in a clear downtrend below its key moving averages on the same risk-off logic.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Fell 1.5% even on a war day — the hawkish Fed minutes and a firmer dollar (higher-for-longer rates) outweighed the safe-haven bid, which flowed into oil instead.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: The day's big mover, +4.6% (Brent pushed toward $80) as the US and Iran traded strikes and Hormuz traffic halted — a full 'war premium' is back in energy.

board.name: USD/INR
board.read: The rupee tanked 59 paise to a record-low 95.55 per dollar — costlier oil means more dollars needed to pay for imports, and a stronger dollar adds pressure.

board.name: VIX (volatility)
board.read: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' jumped ~5% as the war shock lifted hedging demand — still historically low, but rising; India's own volatility gauge spiked far more.

themes.note: The US-Iran war sent WTI +4.6% and Brent toward $80 with Hormuz shipping halted — a big war premium that lifts oil producers but taxes import-heavy economies and fuel-consuming sectors.
The worst day in months — Nifty -2.1%, Sensex -1,677 pts, Bank Nifty -2.5%, rupee at a record low — as the oil shock hit the most oil-import-dependent large market first.
Gold FELL 1.5% despite the war, as hawkish Fed minutes and a firm dollar raised the cost of holding non-yielding metal — the haven bid went to oil, not gold.
The rare bright spot: China easing H200 purchases lifted Nvidia ~3% and helped the Nasdaq claw back to near-flat, bucking the broader risk-off tape.

what_to_watch.title: US and Iran trade strikes — oil spikes and Hormuz shipping halts
key_points: US hit 80+ Iran targets and 60+ Guard boats; Iran hit US bases in the Gulf
Trump: ceasefire 'over,' may hit Iran again; revoked Iran's oil licence
WTI +4.6% to ~$73.7; Brent toward $80; tanker traffic 'essentially stopped'
India, importing ~85% of its oil, is the biggest single loser
what_to_watch.gist: The fragile US-Iran truce broke: after Iran struck three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, the US hit more than 80 targets across Iran and 60-plus Revolutionary Guard boats, Iran fired back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over.' US crude jumped ~4.6% to ~$73.7 and Brent pushed toward $80 as tanker traffic through the strait stopped.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Oil is the single biggest lever on India's whole economy — it feeds petrol prices, inflation, the subsidy bill and the rupee. A live war at the chokepoint that carries roughly 40% of India's crude (and most of its gas) is the exact opposite of the cheap-oil tailwind that had powered the market — which is why the Nifty crashed and the rupee hit a record low today.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Until this de-escalates, every Hormuz headline moves Indian stocks, the rupee and inflation expectations; a sustained spike is a real hit to India's outlook.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether the US strikes again as Trump threatened, whether Iran tries to close or mine Hormuz, and whether WTI holds above ~$73 or Brent breaks $80.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting off supply, even before any barrel is actually lost.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: For an oil-importing country, a single event at a chokepoint can move the whole market — geography and geopolitics, not just how much is pumped, set the energy price and ripple into inflation, currency and stocks.

what_to_watch.title: India's worst day in months — Sensex -1,677, rupee at a record low
key_points: Bank Nifty -2.51% to 56,743 led the fall
Rupee sank 59 paise to a record-low 95.55/$
India VIX (volatility) spiked sharply as fear jumped
what_to_watch.gist: Indian benchmarks suffered their sharpest selloff in over three months: the Sensex crashed 1,677 points (2.15%) to 76,504 and the Nifty fell 517 points (2.12%) to 23,882, with banks (Bank Nifty -2.5%) leading. The rupee tumbled 59 paise to a record-low 95.55 per dollar as costlier oil and a firmer dollar hit at once.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: This is how tightly India is chained to the oil price. Higher crude widens the import bill (India buys most of its oil in dollars), which weakens the rupee, feeds inflation and gives the RBI less room to cut rates — a chain that hits banks, real estate and consumer stocks. The size of the drop shows how much of the recent rally rested on cheap oil alone.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Expect volatility and possible foreign-investor selling until oil settles; a weaker rupee also raises the cost of imports and dollar debt for Indian companies.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether the Nifty holds 23,800-24,000, foreign-investor (FII) flows, the rupee versus 96, and TCS's after-close results tonight.

key_terms.definition: The index of India's biggest banks; it is rate-sensitive, so it falls when higher oil/inflation reduce the chance of RBI rate cuts.
Money that foreign institutional investors move into or out of Indian stocks; heavy outflows can pull the market down in a risk-off phase.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: The force that drives a rally usually drives the reversal too — an oil-importing market that rose on cheap crude is the same market that falls fastest when a Gulf war sends the price back up.

what_to_watch.title: TCS opens earnings season tonight — the first reality-check
key_points: Q1 FY27 results after close today; earnings call at 7pm IST
Revenue seen ~Rs 72,012 cr (+1.9% QoQ); dollar revenue ~$7.6bn, ~flat
Profit seen dipping ~2.5% QoQ as wage hikes squeeze margins
Interim dividend expected; record date 15 July
what_to_watch.gist: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), India's largest IT-services firm, reports June-quarter results after the close today, with a 7pm IST call. Analysts expect revenue near Rs 72,012 crore (up ~1.9% quarter-on-quarter), roughly flat dollar revenue (~$7.6bn), and profit slipping ~2.5% as annual wage hikes weigh on margins — plus a likely interim dividend.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: TCS opens the season and sets the tone for the whole IT sector, which has been under a cloud over whether AI will disrupt it or drive new demand. Landing on the market's worst day in months, a reassuring print could steady nerves while a soft one would deepen the gloom — and its AI commentary matters most of all.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Strong deal wins and upbeat AI commentary would lift Infosys, HCLTech and Wipro and support sentiment; a miss would hit IT hardest just as the Iran shock bites.
what_to_watch.watch: Revenue growth, the operating-margin number, large-deal wins, banking (BFSI) demand, the dividend, and AI commentary — on tonight's 7pm call.

key_terms.definition: The share of revenue left as profit after running costs — a key gauge of how efficiently a company turns sales into earnings.
India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026; Indian firms report on an April-March financial year.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: The first big earnings report of a season carries outsized weight — it resets expectations for every peer that reports after it, so one company's numbers can move an entire sector.

what_to_watch.title: Hawkish Fed minutes firm the dollar — and gold falls even on a war day
key_points: June minutes: unanimous 12-0 hold at 3.5%-3.75%
Split committee; many saw rates ending 2026 higher
Gold fell 1.5% to ~$4,084 as the dollar firmed
Next signal: the 29 July Fed rate decision
what_to_watch.gist: The Fed's first minutes under new chair Kevin Warsh showed a committee that held rates unanimously but split on what's next — with many members expecting rates to end 2026 above today's level and inflation worries 'mounting.' That hawkish tilt firmed the dollar and helped push gold down 1.5% to ~$4,084 even as war fears raged.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: This is a live lesson that gold isn't an automatic 'war hedge.' Higher-for-longer rates raise the opportunity cost of holding metal that pays no interest, and a stronger dollar makes dollar-priced gold pricier abroad — so on a day the oil-driven haven bid went into crude, gold actually fell. A firm dollar also keeps the rupee soft and foreign flows cautious toward India.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Don't assume gold rises in every crisis — watch the dollar and real yields; a hawkish Fed can override the fear bid, and it keeps pressure on rate-sensitive assets everywhere.
what_to_watch.watch: The 29 July Fed rate decision, the dollar index, and whether the oil spike pushes the next US inflation print high enough to revive hike talk.

key_terms.definition: What you give up by choosing one option over another — here, the interest you forgo by holding non-yielding gold instead of a bond.
A stance that favours higher interest rates to fight inflation, even at the cost of slower growth — the opposite of 'dovish.'

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A safe haven is only 'safe' in the right conditions — gold competes with cash and bonds, so when rates and the dollar rise, even a war can fail to lift it. Watch what an asset does, not just what it's called.

what_to_watch.title: The one bright spot: China cracks the door to Nvidia's H200
key_points: China to let Alibaba, ByteDance, DeepSeek buy limited H200 chips
A softening of strict controls on advanced US AI chips
Nvidia jumped ~3% to ~$203, bucking the red tape
Helped the Nasdaq claw back to near-flat
what_to_watch.gist: A report that China will let its top AI firms buy a limited amount of Nvidia's H200 chips lifted Nvidia about 3% to ~$203 — one of the few gainers on a day the broad US market fell. It marks a thaw in China's earlier reluctance to buy the coveted US hardware, and would help ease a global AI-chip shortage.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: The AI-chip trade has been the market's load-bearing wall, so anything that widens Nvidia's biggest potential market is a genuine positive for the whole AI complex — and a rare offset to the war-driven risk-off mood. It also read through to Indian IT sentiment, which rides the same AI-demand story into TCS's results.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: A friendlier China channel supports Nvidia and AI-infrastructure names, but the approvals are 'limited' and case-by-case, so treat it as a crack in the door, not a re-opening.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether the approvals are formalised and how large the quotas are, plus any US pushback on easier H200 sales to China.

key_terms.definition: Government limits on selling sensitive technology (like advanced chips) to certain countries, usually on national-security grounds.
A high-end Nvidia AI chip — powerful but a step below its very top models — sought after for training AI systems.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: In a market led by one theme, news that widens that theme's addressable market can lift a stock even against a falling tape — but policy-driven gains are only as durable as the policy behind them.

global_summary.us: US stocks fell as the US-Iran escalation and rising oil weighed — the Dow dropped ~1% (~500 points) and the S&P slipped 0.36%, while the Nasdaq clawed back to near-flat (-0.08%), cushioned by a ~3% jump in Nvidia on news China will allow limited H200 chip purchases.
global_summary.india: The worst day in over three months: the Nifty crashed 2.1% to 23,882 and the Sensex sank 1,677 points to 76,504, with banks leading (Bank Nifty -2.5%) as the oil shock hit an import-dependent market; the rupee fell 59 paise to a record-low 95.55/$. TCS reports Q1 tonight.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin fell ~1.9% to ~$62,096 toward a one-year low and Ether -1.9%, dragged by the risk-off mood, persistent ETF outflows and a Fed that won't cut — crypto still trading as a risk asset, not a haven.
global_summary.macro: WTI crude jumped ~4.6% to ~$73.7 (Brent toward $80) as the US and Iran traded strikes and Hormuz traffic halted; gold fell 1.5% to ~$4,084 on a hawkish Fed and firm dollar, and the VIX rose ~5% to ~16.9.

catalysts_today.event: TCS Q1 FY27 results + interim dividend; call 7pm IST
Rupee at record low; RBI intervention watch
AI-chip trade after China H200 easing report

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: Rose +1.08% to $314.03 (from $310.66) even as the war shock dragged the broad market down — a classic 'flight to quality' where money hides in the biggest, steadiest names. It pushed to a fresh record-area high and stayed firmly in its uptrend, rated Strong Buy.

news.headline: US stocks slip as US-Iran strikes lift oil; Nvidia bucks the fall

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$4.6T) in Technology at a rich trailing P/E of ~38 (forward P/E ~32 signals expected profit growth). Revenue is growing ~17% a year and it keeps ~27 cents of every sales dollar as profit; beta ~1.1 means it moves roughly with the market. Dividend yield ~0.3%.
favorites.technicals: Healthy uptrend, rated Strong Buy: price sits above both its 50-day (~$298) and 200-day (~$273) averages. RSI ~63 is firm but not yet overbought, leaving some room to run, though it is near the top of its 52-week range after a fast climb — a caution flag for chasing it here.
favorites.catalysts: Fiscal Q3 earnings later this month; product-cycle and AI-feature news; broad US tech sentiment and the 29 July Fed decision.
favorites.risks: Trading near record highs leaves it exposed to any risk-off deepening; a daily close back below the ~$298 50-day average would warn the uptrend is stalling. A firm dollar and a slowing US consumer are slow headwinds.

favorites.name: NVIDIA Corp.
favorites.whats_new: The day's standout — Nvidia jumped +3.11% to $203.05 (from $196.93) after a report that China will let its top AI firms buy limited H200 chips, and its technical rating was UPGRADED from Sell to Buy as RSI rose from ~44 to ~50. It's climbing back toward its 50-day line and out of laggard status.

news.headline: China to let top AI firms buy limited Nvidia H200 chips, report says

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$5.0T) in Semiconductors, trailing P/E ~30 with a much lower forward P/E of ~15 — the market expects earnings to keep surging (revenue growing ~85% a year, keeping ~63 cents of every sales dollar as profit). Beta ~2.2 makes it far more volatile than the market; dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Transitional / sideways, now rated Buy: price ($203) is between its 200-day (~$191) and 50-day (~$210) averages, so reclaiming the ~$210 50-day line would be the next bullish signal. RSI ~50 is neutral. The China news is a fundamental catalyst; watch it hold above the ~$191 200-day support that defines its longer trend.
favorites.catalysts: Formalisation of China's H200 approvals, the direction of the US AI/chip trade, and its own next earnings — any AI-demand headline moves it fast given its high beta.
favorites.risks: High beta means outsized swings in a risk-off tape; the gain rests partly on a policy report that could reverse. A decisive break below the ~$191 200-day line would be bearish.

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Slid ~1.3% to about Rs 1,275.9 (day range ~Rs 1,271.6-1,302.3) in the broad selloff, dropping to a fresh 52-week-low zone. The oil spike is a mixed blessing for a refiner — higher crude can squeeze refining margins even as it's an energy name. (Note: today's automated feed returned no fresh price for Indian tickers, so this uses the cited 8 July market close.)

news.headline: Sensex crashes 1,677 pts, Nifty ends at 23,882 on US-Iran tension

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~Rs 17T) in Energy at a moderate trailing P/E of ~22 (forward P/E ~18 implies expected growth). Revenue is growing ~12% a year and it keeps ~8 cents of every sales dollar as profit; beta ~0.18 makes it much steadier than the market. Dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend near 52-week lows: the stock has been sliding below its 50-day (~Rs 1,335) and 200-day (~Rs 1,415) averages, and RSI ~48 is neutral. Support to hold is the recent ~Rs 1,272 low; a close back above ~Rs 1,335 would be the first sign of a turn. The broad-market crash and oil-margin worries are the near-term overhang.
favorites.catalysts: Crude and refining-margin trends, any news on retail/Jio/new-energy verticals, and broad India market direction through earnings season.
favorites.risks: Trading at fresh 52-week lows in a confirmed downtrend; a break below ~Rs 1,272 support opens more downside. Higher crude can pressure refining economics, and a weak overall market weighs on the heaviest index stock.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: Fell another -1.9% to ~$62,096, sliding toward fresh one-year lows as the war-driven risk-off tape, steady ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed all weighed. Its technical rating slipped to Sell (from Neutral), confirming the downtrend rather than a dip to buy.

news.headline: Bitcoin under pressure as US-Iran escalation lifts oil

favorites.fundamentals: The largest cryptocurrency by value (~$1.24T market cap). Bitcoin pays no yield, so its appeal falls when 'safe' bond returns are high — and a Fed that has ruled out 2026 cuts keeps those yields attractive versus non-yielding crypto.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend, rated Sell: price is below both its 50-day (~$65,600) and 200-day (~$74,000) averages, near the bottom of its 52-week range, with RSI ~46. It lost the ~$62k support area; a decisive break risks a fresh leg lower, while reclaiming ~$63k would be an early stabilisation sign.
favorites.catalysts: US spot-ETF flow data, the 29 July Fed decision, and the risk-on/off mood in AI/tech that crypto now tracks closely.
favorites.risks: Near a one-year low with the downtrend intact; persistent institutional ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed are the main overhangs, and a risk-off market keeps pressure on speculative assets.

risks: Oil/war shock: an escalating US-Iran conflict or any move to close Hormuz would push crude higher, widen India's import bill, pressure the rupee and revive inflation — the biggest cross-book risk today.
Rupee weakness: at a record-low 95.55/$, further falls raise import and dollar-debt costs and can trigger foreign-investor selling and RBI intervention.
Earnings disappointment: a soft TCS print tonight could puncture the IT sector and deepen the selloff on an already-fragile day.
Hawkish Fed: minutes showed inflation worries and possible hikes — a firmer dollar keeps the rupee soft, pressures gold and rate-sensitive tech, and caps any relief rally.
Reversal risk in relief trades: Nvidia's bounce rests partly on a policy report that could reverse, and a deeper risk-off wave would drag even resilient mega-caps like Apple.
